content.full_text:
State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
Advice Files 
Date 
12/3/83 
From 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
J.Pritchard 
Subiect: 
Referral Fees 
It was determined at the advice request meeting 
on Dec. 1, 1983, that "referral fees" paid by a lobbyist 
or a retained entity to persons who have referred lobbying 
clients to them, are reportable payments pursuant to 
Gov. Code Section 82045. 


STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
CALIFORNIA WASTE MANAGEMENT BOARD 
1 020 NINTH STREET, SUITE 300 
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 
9581" 
(916) 322-3330 
November 21, 1983 
Councilwoman Joy picus 
City Hall, Room 281 
200 North Spring Street 
Los Angeles, CA 
90012 
Attention: 
Sharon Schuster 
Dear Councilwoman picus: 
GEORGE DEUICMEJIAN, Go"'fmOl' 
On October 26, 1983, Sharon Schuster of your staff requested that I check 
our meeting records to see if the Board had heard any matters involving 
Browning Ferris Industries and members' of the Greater Los Angeles Solid 
Waste Management Association (GLASWMA). 
I understood this request to be 
(Concerned ,pith the prohibition from ncceptir.g polll..J.l,..;dl contributions 
contained in Chapter 1049, statutes of 1982 CAB 1040, Levine), which 
enacted Government Code Section 84308. 
I checked the Board packets for tile meetings of October 13.,.14, 
September 15-16, August 18-19, and July 21",",22. 
To my knowledge, neither 
Browning Ferris Industries nor GLASWMA members had matters before the 
Board at these meetings~ 
We do not know now which firms regulated by the Board may appear in 
future months. 
Please be aware that the above-noted law also requires 
that your contributors, as well as you! disclose their contributions to 
the Board for one year, when they come before the Board. 
We will provide you with a computer run of permi'tted facilities, and 
operator and owner names, against which you may check your contributor 
list. 
With regard to the GLASWMA PAC contribution, I was advised November 18, 
1983, by Attorney Diane Fishburn with the Fair Political Practices Commis-
sion that contributions from bona fide organizations or PACs do not 
trigger the need to know or disclose the names' of individual members. 
Concerning your responsibility regarding contributions from individua'ls't 
whose business ties are unknown to yo~, who may have applications before 
the Board, Title 14, California Administrative Code, Section 18438.7(c) 
provides guidance. 
This is one of the sections which implements Govern-
ment Code Section 84308. 
The effect of this regulatlon is that the 


State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
: Advice Request Meeting 
11/30/83 
From 
; 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Jeanne 
Subject: Question from the Franchise Tax Board: 
Reporting by 
Retained Entities 
The FTB is auditing a retained entity. 
The retained 
entity reports 100% of its disbursements. 
It had disburse-
ments to the IRS, FTB and Social Security which represented 
the employees' and employer's share of contributions and 
taxes, and the employees' withholding. 
The retained entity did not include these disbursements 
as overhead. 
The auditors feel that these disbursements 
should be reported as overhead. 
The auditor's position is that these payments must be 
reported because all disbursements must be reported. 
The employer's position is that the largest portion of 
these payments (employees' share of contributions and withholding) 
have already been reported as part of gross salary. 
It seems to me that if any part of these payments is 
reportable, it is only the employer's share (since the 
employee's share and withholding are included in gross salary). 
The FTB's problem is that they have no way of determining 
what part of the disbursement is the employees' share and what 
part is the employer's share, without looking at their income 
tax records, which FTB is not supposed to do. 
One solution would be to not require reporting of the 
employer's share at all. 
That way, each time the auditors 
see a payments to IRS, FTB, Social Security, etc., they will 
know that the reportable portion has been reported as gross 
salary. 
If we feel that the empoloyer's share is reportable under 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section l8620(d) (2) (C), there must be some way 
for the employer to indicate which portion of the lump sum 
disbursement represents the employer's share, so only that 
portion is reported as overhead. 
In any event, we probably need to amend Section 18620 
to clarify whether these payments are reportable.

sections.conclusion: It was determined at the advice request meeting on Dec. 1, 1983, that "referral fees" paid by a lobbyist or a retained entity to persons who have referred lobbying clients to them, are reportable payments pursuant to Gov. Code Section 82045.
sections.question_synthetic: The document addresses multiple legal questions: (1) whether referral fees paid by lobbyists to persons who referred clients are reportable payments, and (2) whether retained entities must report employer tax contributions and withholdings as overhead or if employee portions already included in gross salary should be excluded.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC determined that referral fees paid by lobbyists or retained entities to persons who referred clients are reportable payments under Government Code Section 82045. Regarding tax withholdings, the FPPC suggested that employer's share of contributions may be reportable, but clarification and possible amendment to Section 18620 is needed to resolve the reporting ambiguity.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple FPPC memoranda without traditional Q/A section headers. First memorandum (83A270) has clear conclusion about referral fees. Second memorandum (11/30/83) presents analysis of tax withholding reporting without explicit final conclusion. Included is also an unrelated letter from California Waste Management Board regarding political contribution disclosures. Confidence reduced due to mixed document types and lack of traditional section headers.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The document addresses multiple legal questions: (1) whether referral fees paid by lobbyists to persons who referred clients are reportable payments, and (2) whether retained entities must report employer tax contributions and withholdings as overhead or if employee portions already included in gross salary should be excluded.

CONCLUSION: It was determined at the advice request meeting on Dec. 1, 1983, that "referral fees" paid by a lobbyist or a retained entity to persons who have referred lobbying clients to them, are reportable payments pursuant to Gov. Code Section 82045.

embedding.summary: This document contains two FPPC memoranda addressing reportability issues: one establishing that referral fees are reportable under Gov. Code Section 82045, and another analyzing the proper reporting treatment of employer and employee tax contributions and withholdings by retained entities.
